claim.content: For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=1, extrapolating finite-N quasiparticle effective masses m^*(N) produced by quartic fits to DMC energy bands and fitting m^*(N)=m^*(∞)+b N^{-γ} yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.955(2) in hartree atomic units (a.u.), where m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} with k_F the Fermi wave vector of the infinite system.

conclusion.content: For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=1, extrapolating finite-N quasiparticle effective masses m^*(N) produced by quartic fits to DMC energy bands and fitting m^*(N)=m^*(∞)+b N^{-γ} yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.955(2) in hartree atomic units (a.u.), where m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} with k_F the Fermi wave vector of the infinite system.

premises.content: When extrapolating finite-system quasiparticle effective masses m^*(N) to the thermodynamic limit with the model m^*(N)=m^*(∞)+b N^{-γ}, fixing the exponent γ=3/2 as a plotting choice (i.e., plotting m^* against N^{-3/2} and performing a linear fit) produces a visually consistent linearization of the data for the range of N values studied and yields extrapolated m^*(∞) values close to those obtained when γ is allowed to vary, but this choice is not derived from first principles for the finite-Δk quartic-fitting procedure used here and therefore introduces model-selection uncertainty into the extrapolated numbers.

The numerical thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective-mass estimates reported here (for example m^*(∞)=0.955(2) a.u. at r_s=1) are robust approximations to the true thermodynamic-limit values for the ideal two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas only under the assumptions that (i) quartic-band fitting over appropriately chosen finite Δk yields accurate per-N derivatives, (ii) the finite-size dependence over the sampled N values is adequately modeled by a single power law b N^{-γ} with γ near the best-fit values found, and (iii) other systematic errors (time-step bias in total-energy differences, population-control bias, and trial-node fixed-node errors) are negligible compared with the stated statistical uncertainties; violations of any of these assumptions would change the extrapolated numbers beyond the quoted error bars.
      Ref. 20

steps.reasoning: Start from the upstream conclusions that (a) the band-fitting and finite-size extrapolation procedure is the method used to obtain thermodynamic-limit effective masses and (b) finite-$N$ DMC bands at representative system sizes are reliable (these results are established in the prior conclusions identified above); treat the per-size quartic fits and finite-size extrapolation framework as given.

Describe the practical calculation performed for the paramagnetic HEG at density parameter $r_{s}=1$: for each simulated system size $N$ in the paramagnetic set ($N=26,50,74,114,162$ as used in the calculations), fit the DMC band $\mathcal{E}(k)$ to the quartic form $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_{0}+\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\alpha_{4}k^{4}$, evaluate the derivative $(d\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{F}}$ from the fitted polynomial at the Fermi wave vector $k_{F}$ appropriate to the infinite paramagnetic system, and compute $m^{*}(N)=k_{F}/(d\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{F}}$ for each $N$.
Fig. 3

Fit the sequence of finite-$N$ masses $m^{*}(N)$ to the finite-size model $m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\infty)+b\,N^{-\gamma}$, where $m^{*}(\infty)$, $b$, and $\gamma$ are fitting parameters, and perform Monte Carlo sampling of the error bars to obtain uncertainties in the fitted parameters; the paper reports that the extrapolation shown in the principal figures assumes an exponent of $\gamma=3/2$ (i.e., $\gamma=1.5$) for the plotted extrapolation, and Table I summarizes extrapolated values for different assumed $\gamma$.
Fig. 7
Fig. 8
Table I

Report the final numerical extrapolated thermodynamic-limit value obtained for the paramagnetic 2D HEG at $r_{s}=1$: $m^{*}(\infty)=0.955(2)$ in hartree atomic units; this value is presented in the tabulated results and in the paper's conclusions as the extrapolated effective mass after removing the observed systematic finite-size trend.
Table II

motivating_questions.content: For high density ($r_{s}=1$) the accurate thermodynamic-limit value of the paramagnetic effective mass was uncertain because previous calculations had not combined sufficiently small stochastic error bars with controlled finite-size extrapolation. A reliable $m^{*}(\infty)$ at $r_{s}=1$ is needed both as a benchmark for perturbative methods (e.g., $GW$ variants) and to assess whether interaction-induced mass suppression or enhancement occurs at high density, but the lack of precise finite-size-corrected QMC results left this benchmark unavailable.
